description: 12-factor evaluation framework for solo / part-time project ideas. Each factor is scored 1-5 against the rubric anchors. Weights define the Balanced ranking mode; other modes use their own weight vectors in modes.json.

factors.name: Demand Evidence
factors.description: How strong is the evidence that people actually pay for this? Named competitor with public MRR beats generic category claims.

rubric.1: Pure speculation. No named competitors, no traction data.
rubric.2: Adjacent products exist but no direct proof of paid users.
rubric.3: Direct competitors exist with indirect revenue signals (app store presence, funding).
rubric.4: Direct competitors with known public revenue figures (blog posts, press, public MRR).
rubric.5: Concrete named competitor with >$1K MRR publicly cited in the research sources, plus a clear 'X made Y in Z' data point.

factors.name: Build Effort
factors.description: Inverted: less effort = higher score. Solo part-time builders cannot absorb 400h projects.

factors.name: Technical Fit
factors.description: How closely does the required stack match the user's existing comfort stack (from profile.yaml).

rubric.1: Completely new stack (different language or runtime).
rubric.2: New framework in a familiar language (e.g. React Native when user only knows web React).
rubric.4: Pure match with the user's existing stack.
rubric.5: Extends an app the user has already shipped — direct code reuse possible.

factors.name: Distribution Clarity
factors.description: Is there a clear channel to reach customers? Platform-native stores (Chrome Web Store, Figma Community, Raycast) score highest because discovery is built in.

rubric.1: Pure 'build it and they will come' — no channel identified.
rubric.2: SEO + content marketing only, long-tail play.
rubric.3: One clear channel (Reddit, X, Product Hunt) but highly competitive.
rubric.5: Platform-native store plus an existing audience the user already reaches.

factors.name: Asset Leverage
factors.description: How much of the user's existing assets (Replit apps, code, IP, audience) does this project reuse?

rubric.3: Reuses specific patterns / snippets from existing apps.
rubric.4: Extends a specific existing Replit app.
rubric.5: Direct v2 of an existing Replit app (AniRec, weight-game, ThesPlan, etc.).

factors.name: Time-to-First-Revenue
factors.description: How fast can this reach its first paid customer? Fast validation beats theoretical upside for solo builders.

rubric.1: More than 4 months to first paid user (complex B2B sales cycles).
rubric.5: Less than 2 weeks (pre-orders, waitlist monetization, instant ship on store).

factors.name: TAM (Total Addressable Market)
factors.description: Rough global market size estimate. Solo can live in $100M niches; larger TAM is a bonus, not a requirement.

factors.name: Competition Density
factors.description: Curve score: middle is best. 0 competitors = no market. 30+ competitors = commoditized. Sweet spot is 7-12 direct competitors.

rubric.1: Zero competitors (no validated market) OR more than 30 (commoditized).
rubric.5: 7-12 competitors — the sweet spot: validated demand with room to differentiate.

factors.name: Moat
factors.description: How hard is this to copy after launch? Less critical for portfolio strategy than for a single defensible bet.

rubric.1: Pure wrapper anyone can copy in a weekend.
rubric.3: Custom data or workflow integration that would take weeks to replicate.
rubric.4: Network effects OR data accumulation OR deep vertical integration.
rubric.5: Multiple of the above plus high switching costs.

factors.name: Pricing Power
factors.description: What price can this command? Commodity pricing leaves no margin; premium pricing requires a strong wedge.

rubric.4: €20 to €50 per month (prosumer or small B2B).

factors.name: Differentiator Strength
factors.description: Does this have a unique angle, or is it a me-too?

rubric.4: Unique angle plus a skill-based or asset-based moat that is hard to copy.
rubric.5: Category-defining wedge — the new thing no one has built yet.

factors.name: Operational Burden
factors.description: Inverted: less burden = higher score. Solo builders cannot sustain 24/7 support or heavy compliance work.

rubric.2: High-touch support and account management required.
rubric.3: Standard SaaS support plus occasional updates.
rubric.4: Light SaaS, mostly automated, minimal support.
rubric.5: Fire-and-forget — one-time purchase, no backend, no ongoing support.
